name: Cyclone — Safe C with Explicit Regions
summary: Grossman, Morrisett, Jim, Hicks, Wang, Cheney's Cyclone (PLDI 2002) is a safe C dialect with explicit, lexically-scoped region annotations on pointer types. Pointer types int*ρ are parameterised by region variables; a LIFO discipline on region lifetimes induces an outlives relation that becomes a sound subtyping rule. The canonical 'explicit regions, modest annotation cost' reference and direct ancestor of Rust's lifetime annotations.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Grossman, Morrisett, Jim, Hicks, Wang, Cheney's **Cyclone** (PLDI 2002) is a safe C dialect with explicit, lexically-scoped region annotations on pointer types. Pointer types `int*ρ` are parameterised by region variables; a LIFO discipline on region lifetimes induces an **outlives** relation that becomes a sound subtyping rule. Existential region bounds let closures and ADTs hide their captured region while still proving non-dangling.

Cyclone is the canonical "explicit regions, modest annotation cost" reference and the direct ancestor of Rust's lifetime annotations. Status (as of 2026-04): no longer actively developed as a language, but its design vocabulary (region variables, outlives, existential regions) is the lineage that lives on through Rust's borrow checker.

sections.title: Annotation burden and bidirectional checking
sections.content:
The annotation burden was empirically tractable: porting legacy C, only ~6% of an 8% changed-line set were region annotations. Cyclone's algorithm is **bidirectional type-checking with effect inference** — not full Hindley-Milner reconstruction. A LIFO region stack supports static reasoning about which pointer types may safely outlive which scopes; existential region bounds let library types abstract over the region of captured data without exposing it to clients.

The fact that ~94% of changed lines were not region-related is one of the data points cited by language designers when arguing that explicit lifetime annotations need not dominate code.

sections.title: Unique and reference-counted tracked pointers (2006)
sections.content:
Tracked unique/RC pointers were added in the 2006 Science of Computer Programming follow-up paper, extending Cyclone beyond pure region-based reasoning. **Unique pointers** carry a one-of-a-kind ownership obligation — closer in spirit to a linear/affine type — and **reference-counted pointers** add a runtime owner-count for cases where lexical region scope is too restrictive.

This combination — explicit regions for the common case, tracked unique pointers for the cases that don't fit — anticipates the design point Rust later adopted: lifetimes for stack-shaped data, `Box`/`Rc`/`Arc` for cases that need a non-lexical owner.
